# Tohoku Region
Expansive landscapes, rustic hot springs, epic festivals and richtraditions
Prefectures:
* [Aomori](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1202.html)
* [Akita](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1203.html)
* [Iwate](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1204.html)
* [Yamagata](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1205.html)
* [Miyagi](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1206.html)
* [Fukushima](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1208.html)
Prefectures:
* [Aomori](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1202.html)
* [Akita](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1203.html)
* [Iwate](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1204.html)
* [Yamagata](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1205.html)
* [Miyagi](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1206.html)
* [Fukushima](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1208.html)
The Tohoku Region (УМЦkТnХ√, Tōhoku Chihō, literally "North East Region") consists of six prefectures in the north of Japan's largest island, Honshu. It is well known for its countryside, mountains, lakes, [hot springs](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2292.html), high-quality [rice](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2043.html) and rough winters. The Tohoku Region's Pacific coast was hit worst by the earthquake and tsunami of [March 11, 2011](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e5027.html).
